turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS

turns.text: Quickly. Any of the common law, any of the 짯짯 if you go back to the common law and look at instances where aiding and abetting liability, which as this Court pointed out is referenced in 876(b) of the first restatement, that defense was available. Because someone who didn't have that belief was not culpable and, quote, "morally wrong." Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Werbner, you have four minutes left.

turns.name: MARK S. WERBNER
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice. Justice Kagan, to your question just now, I know of no other area of the law where a defendant, if it turns out they're right, they win, and if it turns out they're wrong, they win. That's the position that Cisco is seeking. And the parade of horribles of having to shut down the factory 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: You're argument goes too far, because that's the same for infringement. If you don't think you're infringing and you have a good faith basis, you're right.

turns.name: MARK S. WERBNER
turns.text: Well, there 짯짯 there is the 짯짯 in my mind, the fact is, is that Cisco in their amici pressed Congress to create through the PTO the IPR procedures. It's not a million dollar lawsuit, and it's not one that goes on forever. They have many lawyers that can evaluate the risk and go to the PTO. That's where they should go if they're seeking validity. Infringement you can't go to the PTO, but the IPR allows that.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Of course your response to the 짯짯 the assertion that your 짯짯 your argument goes too far is well, we don't 짯짯 we don't agree that 짯짯 that it applies to whether you've infringed, right? I mean, your argument is even 짯짯 even the question of infringement, despite what we said in Global짯Tech, that is not a defense either, right? So at least you're consistent.

turns.name: MARK S. WERBNER
turns.text: Well, Justice Scalia, I do want to point out that Cisco cannot point to a single statutory provision that suggests that Congress thought that the validity of the patent was an element of 271(b). So, no matter how the Court comes out on the non짯infringement scienter and the Global짯Tech, regardless, our position doesn't depend on it and 짯짯 and there's no statutory support for validity being a component of a 271(b). And the reference was to Blonder짯Tongue? That 짯짯 that case that this Court decided back in, I think, the early '70s said that a jury finding or a judge that a patent is invalid doesn't wipe it out. It's not 짯짯 it doesn't become void or canceled. It's a matter of collateral estoppel that has to be pled under Rule 8(c). And if a defendant later does not, then that patent was infringing an invalid patent because it's an affirmative defense. So I think the Blonder짯Tongue case really completely undermines the position that 짯짯 that infringement presupposes validity. It's quite separate, it's quite distinct and for the reasons we stated, we would ask that the case be reversed and remanded to the Federal circuit unless there are questions. Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
